Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered even though I put in about 20 hours a week. I'd often sit down with good intentions but find myself just going through the motions, reading and re-reading without really absorbing much. I preferred working with written materials—printing things out or typing notes—because that's how information sticks for me. But there was always this underlying sadness that made it hard to feel truly present in what I was doing.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, though not always because of deadlines. Sometimes I'd have these stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to engage, even when nothing specific was due. The anxiety would build up and I'd force myself into marathon study sessions, which weren't healthy but got me through exams. My attendance was near perfect because showing up felt like the one thing I could control, even when everything else felt chaotic inside.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I felt like I should, not because I was excited about it. There was this constant pressure—this anxious voice telling me I was falling behind even when I wasn't. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements or if I'd missed something important. It was more about managing my anxiety than genuine curiosity about the content.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something natural or comfortable for me. I'd go through phases where I'd try to make it a habit, but it always felt like a chore. The mindfulness exercises they recommended helped me cope with the stress of logging in, but they didn't make the platform itself feel any more welcoming or intuitive.
Q5: The reading materials and written transcripts were most useful because that's how I learn best. I could download them, annotate them, and work through them at my own pace offline. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when classmates shared different perspectives, though I often felt too drained to contribute meaningfully myself. Video content was fine but I preferred having the text version.
Q6: I barely touched the video lectures beyond skimming them once. They took too much time and I couldn't highlight or annotate them the way I needed to. Some of the optional interactive activities felt pointless—like busywork that wouldn't actually help me on exams. When you're already struggling with motivation, anything that feels unnecessary gets pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam preparation or if I could see immediate results from completing it. I needed to feel like there was a purpose, a tangible outcome. Vague or open-ended tasks were the hardest because my anxious brain would spiral about whether I was doing them correctly. Clear expectations made things feel manageable.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. I'd feel guilty for not doing them but also resentful that they existed. Required activities had clear stakes—I knew I had to do them. Optional ones just added to this overwhelming sense that I could always be doing more, which fed into my anxiety. I mostly avoided them to protect my mental energy.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual learning happened offline with printed materials, handwritten notes, and textbooks I'd bought separately. I'd download everything I could and work through it away from screens. Reading and writing things out by hand helped me process information better than clicking through online modules ever did. The LMS was just a gateway to get materials, not where real studying happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours with my printed notes, working through concepts, making flashcards, and writing practice essays—none of which showed up anywhere online. Sometimes I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay it mentally while reviewing my written notes offline. My best learning happened in coffee shops with paper and pens, completely disconnected from the platform.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access materials once to download them, then never return to that page on the LMS. Everything I needed lived in folders on my laptop or in printed binders. Going back to the LMS repeatedly felt inefficient and honestly overwhelming given how cluttered the interface was. One download, then offline study—that was my system.
Q12: There were weeks when the sadness was so heavy that even opening my laptop felt impossible. I'd stare at the login page and feel this wave of dread wash over me. My anxiety was high but my energy was low—a terrible combination. During those times, I'd still show up to class physically but mentally I was somewhere else entirely, just trying to survive the day.
Q13: The competing responsibilities were manageable since I had good resources, but the internal battle was exhausting. Fatigue from anxiety and low mood meant that even simple tasks on the LMS felt monumental. I'd log in intending to participate in discussions but end up just scrolling numbly, not really taking anything in. Confusion about assignment requirements amplified everything because I couldn't just ask—that felt too vulnerable.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be completely elsewhere—worrying about things, feeling sad, or just blank. The activity was there technically, but there was no real engagement. It was like my body was going through the motions while my brain had checked out. Those sessions left me feeling even more drained.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low despite my exam scores. The thought of starting something triggered this cascade of anxious thoughts about doing it wrong. I'd tell myself I'd feel better tomorrow, but tomorrow brought the same heaviness. Even knowing something was important didn't override the paralysis I felt. The mindfulness techniques helped sometimes, but not always.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really forced me into action. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. But they also caused intense anxiety spikes—I'd cram everything in at the last moment, which was stressful but weirdly effective for exam preparation. My LMS activity probably looked like nothing for weeks, then sudden bursts right before something was due. It wasn't healthy but it's how I functioned.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Positive feedback felt undeserved somehow, and critical feedback sent me spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades or comments for days because I couldn't handle the emotional response either way. When I did engage with feedback, it was usually late, and by then the moment to improve had passed. I wish I could have been more responsive, but my mental state made it difficult.
Q18: The course structure was actually pretty good—clear modules, organized materials, predictable deadlines. That helped my anxiety somewhat because I knew what to expect. But the structure couldn't account for my internal chaos. Having things laid out clearly didn't mean I could engage with them clearly. The scaffolding was there; I just couldn't always climb it.
Q19: The availability of downloadable materials made engagement easier since I could study in my preferred way. But the discussion requirements felt forced and draining—I'd post because I had to, not because I had something meaningful to say. The video-heavy content was harder for me as a read-write learner. More text-based alternatives would have helped me engage more naturally.
Q20: I think sometimes you're in the LMS because you're anxious and checking things repeatedly, not because you're actually working. Or you're lost and clicking around trying to understand what you're supposed to do. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—it might mean confusion or compulsive checking. For me, less activity often meant I was actually getting work done elsewhere.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam scores and final grade were strong because I studied intensively offline in ways the LMS couldn't track. I understood the material deeply from my reading and writing practice, even though my online engagement looked minimal. The LMS saw a disengaged student; the exams saw someone who knew the content. The disconnect was real.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and timing—when someone logs in, what they click on. But they can't show comprehension, emotional state, or offline effort. They miss the student crying while staring at the screen, or the one who downloaded everything once and mastered it with handwritten notes. Logs capture behavior, not learning. They're a shadow of what's actually happening.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It means being mentally present, curious, and willing to struggle with difficult concepts. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments with my notes, not clicking through online modules. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture. Sometimes I was engaged when it looked like I wasn't, and vice versa.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed materials, or the emotional labor of managing anxiety just to open my laptop. It wouldn't show how the mindfulness intervention helped me cope but didn't fix the underlying sadness. It wouldn't capture the disconnect between my capabilities and my ability to demonstrate them online. My engagement was real—it just didn't live where they were looking.
